html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初編 - First compilation or first edition, used to denote the first volume or edition of a work.
  • 自序 - Author’s preface or preface by the author.
  • 吳蘭雪序 - Preface by Wu Lanxue, a proper name of a person.
  • 重刻耳食录序 - Preface to the reprinted *Ear Feasts*, indicating a new edition or reprint of the text.
  • 吳山锡序 - Preface by Wu Shanxi, another proper name.
  • 耳食录卷一 - Volume One of *Ear Feasts*, a classical Chinese book title.
  • 夕芳 - A title of a story or chapter, likely a poetic or symbolic name.
  • 邓无影 - Deng Wuying, a proper name, possibly a character in the story.
  • 云阳鬼 - The Ghost of Yunyang, a title that includes a place name and a supernatural element.
  • 石室虎 - The Tiger of the Stone Chamber, a title indicating a story involving a tiger in a stone chamber.
  • 青州贾 - The Merchant of Qingzhou, a title with a place name and a profession.
  • 张将军 - General Zhang, a title or name of a character.
  • 方比部 - Fang Bibo, a proper name, possibly a title or official position.
  • 樊黑黑 - Fan Heihei, a proper name, possibly a character with a symbolic name.
  • 谭襄敏夫人 - Madam Tan Xiangmin, a title for a noblewoman or historical figure.
  • 蕊宫仙史 - The Celestial Historian of the Mei Palace, a poetic or symbolic title.
  • 刘秋崖 - Liu Qiuyá, a proper name.
  • 煤夫 - Coal Carrier or Coal Porter, a term for a laborer.
  • 钱氏女 - The Daughter of the Qian Family, a title referring to a woman from a family.
  • 邻虎 - The Neighbor Tiger, a title possibly indicating a story about a tiger near a village.
  • 胡好好 - Hu Huhao, a proper name, possibly with a symbolic or colloquial meaning.
  • 梦中宾主 - Host and Guest in a Dream, a title suggesting a dream narrative.
  • 西村颜常 - Yan Chang of Xicun, a proper name or title.
  • 上床鬼 - The Ghost Who Climbs onto the Bed, a title with a supernatural and domestic element.
  • 红裳女子 - The Woman in the Red Robe, a title with a vivid image.
  • 牛豕瘟鬼 - The Pestilence Ghost of Cattle and Pigs, a title involving disease and animals.
  • 雪媒 - Snow媒, possibly a metaphor or symbolic name, with *媒* meaning 'matchmaker' or 'agent'.
  • 英巨山神 - The God of Yinger Mountain, a title involving a deity and a place.
  • 佑清寺僧 - The Monk of Youqing Temple, a title with a place and a profession.
  • 无赖子 - The Rascal or The Wretch, a derogatory or colloquial term.
  • 余老人 - The Old Man Yu, a proper name.
  • 文慧掸师 - Master Wenhui, the Dharma-Dispenser, a title for a Buddhist monk or teacher.
  • 蜀商 - The Merchant of Shu, a title involving a place and a profession.
  • 毛生 - Mao Sheng, a proper name, possibly a scholar or character.
  • 卖酥饼者 - The Seller of Buttered Cakes, a title referring to a tradesperson.
  • 张小姐 - Miss Zhang, a title for a young woman.
  • 三官神 - The Three Officials Deity, a reference to a traditional Chinese deity.
  • 邹忠介公 - Public Official Zou Zhongjie, a title for a historical or fictional official.
  • 市中小儿 - The Children of the Market, a title referring to common people or children.
  • 长春苑主 - The Master of Changchun Garden, a title involving a garden and a position.
  • 梅花美人 - The Beauty of the Plum Blossom, a poetic or symbolic title.
  • 紫钗郎 - The Young Man with the Purple Hairpin, a title with a poetic or romantic connotation.
  • 楚材 - Chu Cai, a proper name or title.
  • 画师 - The Painter, a title for an artist.
  • 荆州女 - The Woman of Jingzhou, a title with a place and a person.
  • 竹冠道人 - The Daoist with the Bamboo Hat, a title for a hermit or spiritual figure.
  • 霄葬妇 - The Woman Buried in the Sky, a poetic or symbolic title.
  • 望都宰 - The Magistrate of Wangdu, a title for an official.
  • 上官完古 - Shangguan Wangu, a proper name.
  • 报仇蛇 - The Snake of Revenge, a title with a narrative and symbolic meaning.
  • 徐太保 - Grand Guardian Xu, a title for a high-ranking official or noble.
  • 紫溪洞长 - The Master of Zixi Cave, a title involving a cave and a position.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《耳食录》 - *Ear Feasts* is the title of a classical Chinese collection of strange tales and stories, likely modeled after *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio* (*Liaozhai Zhiyi*). The title suggests stories that are heard or passed down orally.
  • 乐钧 - Le Jun (1792–1866), a Qing dynasty scholar and writer known for his short stories and essays.
  • 吴兰雪 - Wu Lanxue, likely a contemporary or friend of Le Jun, who contributed a preface.
  • 重刻 - 'Reprinted' or 'Reissued,' indicating that this is a later edition of the text.
  • 吴山锡 - Wu Shanxi, another contributor of a preface, possibly a scholar or literary figure.
  • 耳食录 - As noted above, a collection of stories, likely involving supernatural, moral, or historical themes.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 耳食录 - A term referring to a collection of tales or anecdotes, likely of a supernatural or fantastical nature. The word 耳食 (ear-food) is a metaphor for stories heard or passed down, rather than those read or studied. 录 (lu) means 'record' or 'collection.' So, 耳食录 can be translated as 'Record of Heard Tales' or 'Collection of Ear-Food Stories.'
  • 二编 - This refers to a second edition or a second volume of a series. It may indicate a continuation or supplement to the original 耳食录.
  • 自序 - A 'self-introduction' or 'preface' written by the author, often explaining the purpose or background of the work.
  • 目录 - A 'table of contents' or 'index,' listing the entries or stories contained in the work.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 耳食录 - This is a title of a classical Chinese collection of tales. The term 耳食 is a metaphor for stories that are passed down by word of mouth, as opposed to those that are written or recorded in books. This suggests that the 耳食录 is a collection of oral or anecdotal stories, possibly of a supernatural or moral nature, akin to Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio (Liaozhai Zhiyi) or other classical Chinese ghost and strange tale collections.
  • 二编 - This indicates that this is the second edition or second volume of the 耳食录, which may have been compiled at a later date or as a continuation of the original.
  • 自序 - This is the author's preface, which is common in classical Chinese literature. It may provide the author's motivation, method, or purpose for compiling the work.
  • 目录 - A table of contents listing the stories or entries in the work. This is typical in classical Chinese collections, especially those of a narrative or anecdotal nature.
  • 武侯碑 - Wu Hou Bei (The Stele of Wu Hou) refers to a monument or inscription honoring Zhuge Liang (Wu Hou), a famous strategist and chancellor of the Shu Han state during the Three Kingdoms period. This may be a story involving a historical figure or a supernatural event tied to this monument.
  • 西坡逸叟 - Xi Po Yi Shou ('The Recluse of Xi Slope') may be the pseudonym or name of the author of the 耳食录二编, or it may refer to a character in one of the stories.
  • 书吏 - Shu Shi refers to a clerk or scribe, often associated with bureaucratic or legal contexts. This may be a character in one of the stories, or it may indicate the type of story being described.

Proper Nouns

  • 耳食录二编 - The title of the work, which should be translated consistently as 'Ear-Food Record: Second Edition' or 'Record of Heard Tales: Second Edition.'
  • 自序 - A title; should be translated as 'Preface' or 'Author’s Introduction.'
  • 目录 - A title; should be translated as 'Table of Contents.'
  • 武侯碑 - Wu Hou Bei is a historical reference to Zhuge Liang. Should be translated as 'The Stele of Wu Hou' or 'The Monument to Zhuge Liang.'
  • 西坡逸叟 - Xi Po Yi Shou may be a pseudonym or character name. Should be translated as 'The Recluse of Xi Slope.'
  • 书吏 - Shu Shi is a title or role; should be translated as 'Clerk' or 'Scribe.'

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 耳食录 - Collection of stories or records, often with a connotation of hearsay or anecdotal nature.
  • 狼狈 - Can mean 'in a difficult situation' or 'in a tangle'; in context, it might refer to a specific story or character.
  • 自序 - Introduction or preface to a work.
  • 搜神志怪，噫吁诞哉 - Collection of strange and fantastic stories.
  • 天地大矣，万物赜矣 - Refers to the vastness of the universe and the complexity of all things.
  • 仆鄙人也，羁栖之暇，辄敢操觚迫记所闻 - Author's self-deprecating introduction and motivation for writing.

Proper Nouns

  • 魏翁 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 女湘 - Proper name, likely a woman's name or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 齐福喜 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 何生 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 柏秀才 - Proper name, likely a scholar or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 龙虱 - Term that could refer to a type of insect or specific character, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 华广 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 陈著 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 张碧云 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 铁丸 - Term that could refer to a type of object or specific character, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 庐山僧 - Term that could refer to a monk from Mount Lu, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 鼪 - Term that could refer to a type of animal or specific character, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 韩五 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 易内 - Term that could refer to a change or specific event, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 石先生 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 疯道人 - Term that could refer to a mad Taoist or specific character, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 恶蝇 - Term that could refer to a type of fly or specific character, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 宋先生 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 金陵樵者 - Term that could refer to a woodcutter from Jinling (Nanjing), requiring contextual understanding.
  • 龙某 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 徐元直 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 沈璧 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 余时鏸 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 章五 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 王黄胡子 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 猫犬 - Term that could refer to cats and dogs, but in context, it might refer to a specific story or character.
  • 仍吉 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 洪四大王 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 石孝廉 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.

  • 庐山怪 - Term that could refer to a strange phenomenon or specific character, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 戴公 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 心疾 - Term that could refer to a mental illness or specific event, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 痴女子 - Term that could refer to a foolish woman or specific character, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 恶鼠 - Term that could refer to a type of rat or specific character, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 忘误 - Term that could refer to a mistake or specific event, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 虾蟆作雹 - Term that could refer to a specific event or story, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 水先生 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.
  • 陶金铃 - Proper name, likely a person or title, requiring contextual understanding.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乾隆壬于夏日 - Refers to the summer of the year 1762 (the first year of the Qianlong era, known as 'Ren Wu' in the sexagenary cycle).
  • 临川乐钧元淑甫撰 - Indicates that the text was written by Le Jūn, also known as Yuan Shu Fu, from Linchuan (modern-day Jiangxi province).
  • 天下至文，本无定质 - A philosophical statement meaning 'the finest writings in the world inherently lack a fixed form.'
  • 譬诸夕霞布空，倏忽异态 - A simile comparing excellent writing to evening clouds that change forms rapidly.
  • 飞英绣水，纵横成章 - Another simile likening writing to embroidered water that forms patterns in all directions.
  • 要须自出机杼，为一家言 - Emphasizes the need for originality and unique expression.
  • 虽墨卿游戏，三味可参 - Suggests that even if the writing is playful, it can still be meaningful and enjoyable.
  • 不必高文典册始克与金石并寿也 - Implies that such writing does not need to be formal or official to endure.
  • 以粲花之笔，抒镂雪之思 - Uses metaphors to describe the friend's writing as vivid and intricate.
  • 事多出于儿女缠绵、仙鬼幽渺 - Indicates that the stories in the collection often involve romantic entanglements and supernatural elements.
  • 间山里巷谐笑助其波澜 - Suggests that the collection also includes humorous anecdotes from the countryside.
  • 胸情所寄，笔妙咸轃 - Means that the writing reflects the author's inner feelings and is skillfully crafted.
  • 虽古作者无多让焉 - Implies that the friend's writing is on par with that of ancient masters.
  • 隋璧汉珠，荫映山海 - A metaphor comparing the friend's writing to valuable treasures that shine brightly.
  • 岂藉誉者以增重哉 - Questions whether the writing needs praise to gain value.
  • 良以寄兴偶同，寓言多感 - Explains that the writing shares common themes and evokes many emotions.
  • 梦簧褥友，乐奏先声 - Describes the writing as melodious and evocative.
  • 残署初退，兀坐紫藤之荫 - Sets the scene of the author sitting under a wisteria tree after a summer heatwave.
  • 追忆旧闻，手草数则 - Indicates that the author is recalling past events and writing them down.
  • 还以质之作者 - Suggests that the author is seeking feedback from the original writer.
  • 重刻耳食录序 - Refers to a reissued preface for *Ersi Lu*.
  • 辛巳之岁，其于浚重为刊板 - Indicates that the reissued version was published in the year 1801 (the year 'Xin Si' in the sexagenary cycle).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乾隆壬于夏日 - Refers to the summer of 1762 during the Qianlong era of the Qing dynasty.
  • 临川 - A historical county in Jiangxi province, known for its cultural significance.
  • 乐钧元淑甫 - The name of the author, Le Jūn, also known as Yuan Shu Fu.
  • 隋璧汉珠 - Refers to valuable treasures from the Sui and Han dynasties, symbolizing the value and beauty of the writing.
  • 梦簧褥友 - A metaphor for the harmonious and evocative nature of the writing.
  • 残署初退 - Refers to the end of the summer heatwave, a common seasonal reference in classical Chinese literature.
  • 紫藤之荫 - Refers to the wisteria tree, often used in classical Chinese poetry and prose to evoke a sense of tranquility and beauty.

Proper Nouns

  • 乾隆 - The reign title of Emperor Qianlong of the Qing dynasty.
  • 壬于 - The first year of the Qianlong era, known as 'Ren Wu' in the sexagenary cycle.
  • 临川 - A historical county in Jiangxi province.
  • 乐钧元淑甫 - The name of the author, Le Jūn, also known as Yuan Shu Fu.
  • 隋璧汉珠 - Refers to valuable treasures from the Sui and Han dynasties.
  • 梦簧褥友 - A metaphor for the harmonious and evocative nature of the writing.
  • 残署 - Refers to the end of the summer heatwave.
  • 紫藤 - Refers to the wisteria tree.
  • 辛巳 - The year 1801, known as 'Xin Si' in the sexagenary cycle.
  • 浚 - Refers to the place where the reissued version was published.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 呜呼！ - Interjection expressing sorrow or lamentation, often translated as 'Alas!' or 'Woe!'
  • 考信必本于六经 - Classical phrase meaning 'To verify truth must be based on the Six Classics.'
  • 著书要归于有用 - Means 'Writing books should ultimately serve a practical purpose.'
  • 《上林》、《子虚》之赋 - Refers to the 'Shanglin' and 'Zixu' rhapsodies, famous works by Sima Xiangru.
  • 导十而惩一 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to guide ten but punish one,' suggesting a policy of leniency.
  • 君子弗尚也 - Means 'the virtuous do not esteem it,' indicating disapproval.
  • 况其下焉者乎 - Rhetorical question meaning 'how much more so for those below them.'
  • 少年绮语之过 - Refers to youthful, ornate speech that is considered a mistake.
  • 学与年而俱进者也 - Means 'learning advances along with age.'
  • 至四十而益工 - Means 'by the age of forty, it becomes even more skillful.'
  • 其辞元本忠孝 - Means 'his words fundamentally embody loyalty and filial piety.'
  • 明达体用 - Means 'clearly understands principles and applications.'
  • 嗟乎！ - Another interjection expressing sorrow or lamentation, similar to 'Alas!'
  • 塞于遇 - Means 'blocked by opportunity.'
  • 绌于年 - Means 'limited by age.'
  • 卒不克一伸其志 - Means 'finally unable to fully express his aspirations.'
  • 世以才于目莲裳 - Means 'the world regards him as talented.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 莲裳 - The pen name of the author being discussed, likely referring to a specific historical figure.
  • 六经 - The Six Classics: the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals), and *Lunyu* (Analects of Confucius).
  • 《上林》、《子虚》之赋 - Famous rhapsodies by Sima Xiangru, known for their elaborate style.
  • 曾宾谷侍郎 - Refers to a specific historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.
  • 两淮 - Refers to the region of Lianghuai, an area in eastern China.
  • 琴樽文史之盛冠于江南 - Describes a cultural scene in Jiangnan, a region known for its literary and artistic achievements.

Proper Nouns

  • 莲裳 - The pen name of the author being discussed.
  • 六经 - The Six Classics.
  • 《上林》、《子虚》 - The 'Shanglin' and 'Zixu' rhapsodies.
  • 曾宾谷侍郎 - A specific historical figure.
  • 两淮 - The region of Lianghuai.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 聊書數語於筒端 - Literally 'to write a few words at the end of a bamboo tube.' Classical expression meaning 'to write a brief note or preface.'
  • 以念世之讀是書考 - 'In order to recall for those who read this book and examine it.' The phrase '念' means 'to recall' or 'to remember,' often used in classical prefaces to address the reader.
  • 知蓮裳之所自喜乃絕不在此 - 'To know that what Lian Shang truly delights in is not in this.' '自喜' means 'what one delights in by oneself' or 'what one personally values.'
  • 當亦蓮裳之所心許焉已 - 'It should also be what Lian Shang has secretly approved of.' '心許' means 'approved of in the heart' or 'secretly approved.'
  • 《山海》徵奇，《齊諧》志怪 - 'The *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas) gathers strange things, the *Qi Heng* (Book of Qi) records strange events.' These are classical texts known for their supernatural and fantastical content.
  • 遐哉尚矣 - A classical expression meaning 'how distant and venerable it is!' Often used to express admiration for ancient works.
  • 矜奇俶诡者 - 'Those who take pride in the strange and unusual.' '矜' means 'to pride oneself,' and '俶诡' means 'unusual' or 'strange.'
  • 所莅之区 - 'The places where they have visited.' '莅' means 'to arrive at' or 'to visit.'
  • 名公巨卿擁彗倒屣 - 'Prominent officials and high-ranking ministers would sweep the floor and reverse their shoes to welcome them.' Classical idiom meaning to welcome someone with great respect and eagerness.
  • 詞客騷人攬環結佩 - 'Poets and literati would gather rings and fasten pendants.' Metaphor for gathering literary or cultural treasures, or engaging in poetic and literary exchanges.
  • 酒酣耳熱 - 'When the wine was flowing and the ears were hot.' Classical expression for being drunk or in a state of intoxication and merriment.
  • 揮麈雄潭 - 'To wave a dust whisk and speak grandly.' Refers to the act of holding a dust whisk (a scholar's tool) and engaging in eloquent, grand discussions.
  • 拈豪伸紙 - 'To take up the brush and write on paper.' Classical way of saying 'to write or compose.'
  • 發為新奇可喜之文 - 'To produce writings that are novel and delightful.' Common expression in classical prefaces to describe the value of literary works.
  • 然非才人不能作也 - 'But only those with talent can do it.' Contrast between the ease of the task and the rarity of the talent required.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道光元年十月 - 'October of the first year of the Dao Guang reign.' Reign title of Emperor Daoguang of the Qing Dynasty (r. 1821–1850). The year 1821 corresponds to the first year of the Dao Guang era.
  • 錢塘徐承思序 - 'Xu Chengsi of Qiantang wrote the preface.' Qiantang is the old name for Hangzhou, a city in Zhejiang province. Xu Chengsi is the author of the preface.
  • 吳山錫敘 - 'Xī Xu of Wu Mountain.' Likely the name of the author or compiler of the text, with 'Wu Mountain' (吳山) referring to a mountain in Hangzhou.
  • 《山海》 - The *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), an ancient Chinese text containing myths, geography, and strange creatures.
  • 《齊諧》 - The *Qi Heng* (Book of Qi), a classical text attributed to the Warring States period, known for collecting strange and supernatural stories.
  • 張茂先《博物志》 - Zhang Maoxian’s *Bomuzhi* (Notes on Things Observed), a classical text on natural history and curiosities.
  • 王子年《拾遺記》 - Wang Zinian’s *Shiyi Ji* (Record of Forgotten Things), a text recording unusual events and ancient legends.
  • 李冗《獨異志》 - Li Rong’s *Duyizhi* (Record of Unique Things), a collection of unusual and extraordinary stories.
  • 趙磷《因話錄》 - Zhao Lin’s *Yinhua Lu* (Notes on Talk), a collection of anecdotes and stories.
  • 孫光憲《北夢瑣言》 - Sun Guangxian’s *Beimeng Suoyan* (Trivial Talk of the Northern Dream), a collection of anecdotes and stories from the Five Dynasties period.
  • 宋永亨《搜采異聞錄》 - Song Yongheng’s *Soucai Yiwén Lu* (Record of Searching for Strange Stories), a text compiling strange and unusual accounts.
  • 夫人寓形宇宙間 - 'Man, as a being, resides in the universe.' Philosophical opening, common in classical Chinese essays.
  • 老死牖下者無論矣 - 'As for those who die old age in the corner of a house, we need not mention them.' Classical way of saying that ordinary people who live unremarkable lives are not worth noting.
  • 懷奇握異之士 - 'Those who hold in their hearts strange and unusual things.' Refers to individuals with extraordinary talents, interests, or experiences.
  • 胸中有萬卷書，足跡行萬里路 - 'With ten thousand volumes in their hearts and ten thousand miles under their feet.' Classical expression emphasizing both intellectual and physical breadth.
  • 名公巨卿 - 'Prominent officials and high-ranking ministers.' Refers to powerful and respected figures of the time.
  • 詞客騷人 - 'Poets and literati.' Refers to those engaged in literary and poetic pursuits.
  • 攬環結佩 - A metaphor for gathering rings and pendants, symbolizing the collection of cultural or literary treasures.
  • 新奇可喜之文 - 'Writings that are novel and delightful.' Common evaluation of literary works in classical Chinese.
  • 才人 - 'Talented individuals,' often referring to poets, writers, or intellectuals.

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 临川乐莲裳先生 - A proper name referring to a specific individual. '临川' is a place name (Nanchang, Jiangxi), and '乐莲裳' is likely a personal name or pen name.
  • 抱沉博瑰丽之才 - A classical expression meaning 'possessing profound, extensive, and splendid talent,' used to describe a person's exceptional abilities.
  • 弱冠后即担铅椠以游历四方 - '弱冠' refers to the age of 20, marking the transition to adulthood. '担铅椠' means 'to carry writing materials,' and '游历四方' means 'to travel around the world.' Together, this phrase describes the individual's early travels and scholarly pursuits.
  • 所过名山大川、通都古迹，一一记之以诗 - '名山大川' refers to famous mountains and rivers, and '通都古迹' refers to major cities and ancient sites. '一一记之以诗' means 'to record them all in poetry,' indicating the individual's habit of documenting his travels through poetry.
  • 出其绪馀，著《耳食录》前后编共二十卷 - '绪馀' means 'leftover' or 'spare time,' and '著' means 'to write.' '《耳食录》' is the title of a work, and '前后编共二十卷' means 'compiled into two volumes, totaling twenty volumes.'
  • 付诸剞劂 - '剞劂' refers to the process of engraving and printing, so this phrase means 'to publish.'
  • 凡生平所闻、所传闻者悉载焉 - '凡' means 'all,' and '悉载焉' means 'are all recorded here,' indicating that the work contains everything the author has heard or heard about.
  • 殆莲裳殁后，版庋多年，间有蠹蚀漫漶而不可辨识者 - '殆' means 'probably,' '殁' means 'to die,' and '版庋多年' means 'the printed copies have been stored for many years.' '间有蠹蚀漫漶而不可辨识者' means 'some have been damaged by insects and become illegible.'
  • 令似滋亭重为刊刻刷布，以彰厥先人之美 - '令似' means 'ordered' or 'commanded,' '滋亭' is likely a proper name, '重为刊刻刷布' means 'to reprint and distribute again,' and '以彰厥先人之美' means 'to honor the beauty of one's ancestors.'
  • 余受而读之 - '余' means 'I,' and '受而读之' means 'received and read it.'
  • 其事之怪怪奇奇，固足赏心骇目 - '怪怪奇奇' means 'strange and wonderful,' and '固足赏心骇目' means 'certainly pleasing to the heart and shocking to the eyes.'
  • 而文章之妙，如云霞变幻、风雨离合 - '文章之妙' means 'the beauty of the writing,' and '如云霞变幻、风雨离合' is a metaphor comparing the writing to the changing clouds and winds, suggesting its dynamic and unpredictable nature.
  • 其悲壮激昂者，真可敲缺唾壶，其缠绵婉丽者，又令人消魂欲死 - '悲壮激昂' means 'sad and grand,' and '敲缺唾壶' is a reference to a story where a man bangs his spitting jar in anger, indicating intense emotion. '缠绵婉丽' means 'tender and graceful,' and '令人消魂欲死' means 'makes one's soul melt and nearly die,' emphasizing the emotional impact.
  • 然阐幽显微，醒愚祛惑之用，即隐寓其中 - '阐幽显微' means 'to reveal the hidden and the subtle,' and '醒愚祛惑之用' means 'to awaken the ignorant and dispel confusion.' '即隐寓其中' means 'is subtly implied within.'
  • 斯乃一片婆心，不可作游戏三昧观也 - '斯乃' means 'this is,' '一片婆心' means 'a heart full of compassion,' and '不可作游戏三昧观也' means 'cannot be seen as a game or trivial matter.'
  • 噫！莲裳虽逝，有子克家，能传播遗书而不使磨灭 - '噫' is an interjection expressing emotion, '莲裳虽逝' means 'although Mr. Lianchang has passed away,' '有子克家' means 'he has a son who can manage the family,' and '能传播遗书而不使磨灭' means 'who can spread his legacy and prevent it from being forgotten.'
  • 则当年著书立说之愿斯可慰矣 - '则' means 'then,' '著书立说之愿' means 'the wish to write books and establish ideas,' and '斯可慰矣' means 'can now be fulfilled.'
  • 刊既成，滋亭丐叙于余 - '刊既成' means 'the publication is complete,' '滋亭丐叙于余' means 'Mr. Ziti asked me to write a preface.'
  • 余学识谫陋，不娴古作，乃钦其孝，勉撰弁语 - '余学识谫陋' means 'my knowledge is shallow,' '不娴古作' means 'I am not familiar with classical works,' '乃钦其孝' means 'but I admire his filial piety,' and '勉撰弁语' means 'and I have written this preface with reluctance.'
  • 无任主臣 - '无任' means 'without limit,' and '主臣' means 'master and servant,' suggesting a humble and respectful tone.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 平江吳山錫並書 - '平江' is a place name (modern-day Pingjiang, Jiangsu or Pingjiang County, Hunan). '吳山錫' is the name of the author or compiler. '並書' means 'and wrote' or 'and composed'.
  • 耳食錄卷一 - '耳食錄' is the title of the work, likely meaning 'Record of Heard Matters' or 'Record of Gossamer Stories' – a genre of anecdotal or hearsay literature. '卷一' is 'Volume One'.
  • 夕芳 - May be the title of a chapter or story. '夕芳' literally means 'evening fragrance' or 'fragrance of dusk,' possibly symbolic or metaphorical in meaning.
  • 邃於學而不偶 - '邃於學' means 'deep in learning' or 'proficient in scholarship.' '而不偶' is an idiom meaning 'without meeting with fortune' or 'without success in life.'
  • 家故貧 - Means 'the family was originally poor' or 'by nature poor.'
  • 一於名露 - This phrase is ambiguous. It may mean 'one (person) named Lu' or 'a child named Lu.' The context suggests that '名露' is a child’s name, so it should be translated as 'a child named Lu.'
  • 伊理以不遇感憤，竟令露廢業 - '不遇' means 'not meeting with fortune' or 'lack of opportunity.' '感憤' is 'to feel resentment or anger.' '竟令露廢業' means 'eventually caused Lu to abandon his studies.'
  • 藉樵牧以奉母三載矣 - '藉' here means 'by means of' or 'through.' '樵牧' refers to 'chopping firewood and herding livestock' – manual labor. '奉母' is 'to support one’s mother.' '三載' is 'three years.'
  • 同邑黃生，伊理舊友也 - '同邑' means 'from the same county' or 'fellow countyman.' '黃生' is 'Mr. Huang' or 'a student named Huang.' '舊友' is 'old friend.'
  • 實起死人而肉白骨 - A classical idiom meaning 'to revive the dead and fatten the bones' – a metaphor for rescuing someone from a desperate situation.
  • 雖然，露之勿學，夫子之志也 - '雖然' is 'although.' '勿學' is 'not to study.' '夫子' is 'the master' or 'the teacher.' '之志也' is 'is his will.'
  • 張吾軍者非露而誰 - '張吾軍' is a name or a title. '非露而誰' is 'who else could it be but Lu?' – a rhetorical question affirming identity.
  • 故人有知，當亦首肯 - '故人' is 'old friend' or 'former companion.' '有知' is 'if they know.' '當亦首肯' is 'they would also agree first.'
  • 試有期矣，偶登城闕 - '試有期矣' is 'the examination is approaching.' '偶登城闕' is 'happened to ascend the city gate' – a poetic way of saying 'he climbed the city wall.'
  • 興發高吟 - '興發' is 'inspiration arose' or 'mood was stirred.' '高吟' is 'to recite or chant loudly.'
  • 儒巾少年神骨清迥 - '儒巾' is a scholar’s cap or a symbol of a scholar. '神骨清迥' is 'spirit and bones clear and refined' – a description of a person with a refined and noble appearance.
  • 前揖之曰 - '前揖之曰' is 'bowed before him and said.'
  • 不減義山《錦瑟》之調 - '不減' is 'not inferior to.' '義山' is a nickname for Li Shangyin, a famous Tang dynasty poet. '《錦瑟》' is the title of one of his poems. '之調' is 'its style' or 'its tune.'
  • 擬議不倫 - '擬議' is 'to compare or liken.' '不倫' is 'not in the same class' or 'unrelated.'
  • 仆固識君，君自不識仆耳 - '仆' is a humble self-reference. '固識君' is 'I certainly know you.' '君自不識仆耳' is 'you yourself do not know me.'
  • 陽謝之而心儀其人 - '陽謝' is 'politely declined' or 'faced with gratitude.' '而' is 'but.' '心儀其人' is 'admired the person in one’s heart.'
  • 漸與浃洽 - '漸與' is 'gradually with.' '浃洽' is 'intimate and harmonious' – a term for deepening friendship or intimacy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 試事 - Refers to the examination process or the event of taking an exam.
  • 程文 - Means 'the essay' or 'the written work,' specifically referring to the exam paper.
  • 恐不諧俗 - Phrase meaning 'fear that it will not conform to popular tastes' or 'be out of step with common expectations.'
  • 盍揣摩時好，以圖一當 - Suggestion meaning 'why not adjust to current preferences in order to succeed?' '盍' is an archaic interrogative particle, '揣摩' means 'to study or adapt,' '時好' refers to 'current tastes,' and '一當' means 'a success' or 'a favorable outcome.'
  • 伯牙不以里耳改弦 - Reference to the legendary musician Bo Ya, who refused to change his playing style even if it was not appreciated by the common people. '改弦' means 'to change one's approach.'
  • 王良不為獲禽易辙 - Reference to Wang Liang, a famous charioteer, who did not alter his driving technique to catch animals. '易辙' means 'to change one's path or method.'
  • 文章不朽之業 - Phrase meaning 'an immortal work of literature' or 'a timeless achievement in writing.'
  • 安敢自贬 - Rhetorical question meaning 'how could I dare to lower my standards?' '安敢' is an emphatic expression of reluctance or refusal.
  • 比晓辞去 - Means 'until morning when he left'; '比' can mean 'until' or 'by,' and '辞去' means 'to take leave.'
  • 數日，杳不復至 - Means 'for several days, he did not return'; '杳' means 'not seen' or 'not heard from.'
  • 試既畢 - Means 'the examination was completed'; '既畢' is a set phrase indicating completion.
  • 袖出露试卷掷案上 - Describes the action of pulling out the essay from one's sleeve and throwing it onto the table. '袖出' means 'to pull out from the sleeve,' and '掷案上' means 'to throw onto the table.'
  • 失色 - Means 'to turn pale' or 'to be shocked,' indicating a strong emotional reaction.
  • 问所从来 - Means 'asked where it came from'; '所从来' is a question about origin.
  • 此岂利器哉 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is this a sharp tool?' '利器' can mean 'sharp tool' or 'effective means.'
  • 吾为君谋易之 - Means 'I have helped you revise it'; '谋易之' means 'to revise or alter.'
  • 已获售矣 - Means 'it has already been accepted'; '获售' means 'to be accepted' or 'to pass.'
  • 意疑 - Means 'was suspicious,' indicating a feeling of doubt.
  • 及视所易卷 - Means 'when he looked at the revised essay'; '所易卷' refers to the altered version.
  • 文甚劣 - Means 'the writing is very poor'; '劣' means 'inferior' or 'poor.'
  • 然心德之 - Means 'but he felt grateful in his heart'; '心德之' means 'to feel grateful inwardly.'
  • 向玉称谢 - Means 'he thanked him'; '称谢' means 'to express thanks.'
  • 逐臭名场者 - Phrase meaning 'one who chases after fame'; '逐臭' is a metaphor for pursuing something distasteful or unworthy.
  • 禄命太薄 - Means 'a thin fate' or 'a limited destiny'; '禄命' refers to one's fortune or destiny.
  • 又得文名 - Means 'and has gained a reputation for literature'; '文名' means 'literary fame.'
  • 恐造物见怒 - Means 'fear that the heavens will be angry'; '造物' refers to the creator or the heavens.
  • 姑为此态 - Means 'temporarily adopt this attitude'; '姑' means 'temporarily' or 'for the time being,' and '为此态' means 'to adopt this behavior.'
  • 先世有清德 - Means 'ancestors had pure virtue'; '清德' means 'pure virtue.'
  • 厥后必大 - Means 'in the future, there will be great success'; '厥后' means 'in the future' or 'later,' and '必大' means 'certainly great.'
  • 愈奇之 - Means 'found him even more remarkable'; '愈' means 'even more,' and '奇之' means 'to find remarkable.'
  • 居数日，别去 - Means 'after several days, he left'; '居数日' means 'after several days,' and '别去' means 'to leave.'
  • 往见黄，未言也 - Means 'went to see Huang but did not speak'; '往见' means 'to go and see,' and '未言也' means 'did not speak.'

  • 严妆袨服，珠翠荧煌 - She was dressed in elaborate attire, adorned with pearls and jade that shone brightly, with '严妆袨服' meaning 'elaborate attire' and '珠翠荧煌' meaning 'adorned with pearls and jade that shone brightly.'
  • 成礼于金碧之堂，同牢于绮罗之席 - The ceremony was held in the golden and jade hall, and they shared a meal on the brocade and silk mat, with '金碧之堂' meaning 'golden and jade hall' and '绮罗之席' meaning 'brocade and silk mat.'
  • 既成眷属，情好笃洽 - After becoming husband and wife, their feelings were deep and harmonious, with '情好笃洽' meaning 'deep and harmonious feelings.'
  • 玉相得之欢，亦更愈于往日 - Jade's joy in being together was even greater than before, with '相得之欢' meaning 'joy in being together.'
  • 甫匝月，露乃告归，将迎妇以觐母 - After a month, Lu then announced his return, intending to bring his wife to meet his mother, with '甫匝月' meaning 'after a month' and '告归' meaning 'announced his return.'
  • 夕芳了无难辞 - Xi Fang had no difficulty in saying goodbye, with '了无难辞' meaning 'had no difficulty in saying goodbye.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夫人出见婿 - This refers to a traditional Chinese wedding ceremony where the bride's mother or another female relative meets the groom for the first time.
  • 玉整衣而出 - 'Jade' is likely a nickname or title for the lady, and '整衣' is a common gesture in traditional Chinese weddings to show respect and readiness.
  • 成礼于金碧之堂，同牢于绮罗之席 - These phrases describe the traditional Chinese wedding ceremony, where the couple is formally married and shares a meal together, symbolizing their union.
  • 既成眷属，情好笃洽 - This refers to the deep and harmonious relationship between the newlyweds, emphasizing the importance of mutual affection and respect in Chinese culture.
  • 甫匝月，露乃告归，将迎妇以觐母 - This describes the custom of the groom bringing his new wife to meet his mother after the wedding, a significant step in the integration of the couple into the family.

  • 夫人 - Lady, referring to the bride's mother or another female relative.
  • 玉 - Jade, likely a nickname or title for the lady.
  • 夕芳 - Xi Fang, likely the name of the bride.
  • 玉父母 - Jade's parents.

  • 至则斜阳一片，秋草离离，愁白杨之悲风，咽空山之流水 - Literal translation: 'When they arrived, they saw a patch of setting sun, scattered autumn grass, the sad wind of the white poplar, and the choked flow of the mountain stream.' This is a vivid and poetic description of the scene.
  • 知逢鬼物，举家惊讶 - Literal translation: 'They knew they had encountered a ghostly being, and the whole family was surprised.' '鬼物' refers to a supernatural being.
  • 露心伤目断，殆亦离魂，踯躅而归，耿耿终日 - Literal translation: 'Lu felt heartbroken and his vision blurred; he seemed to have lost his soul, hesitating on his way back, and remained troubled all day.' '离魂' suggests a state of being detached or lost in thought.
  • 一夜，梦门外簿卤驺从，肩舆中一人乃其父也 - Literal translation: 'One night, he dreamed of officials and servants outside the door, and a person in a sedan chair was his father.' '簿卤驺从' refers to officials and attendants, and '肩舆' is a sedan chair.
  • 谓露曰：“余向馆郑氏，获订姻事。” - Literal translation: 'He told Lu, 'I was staying at the Zheng household and arranged a marriage.' '向馆' means to stay as a guest.
  • 今年蒙上帝授地府司宪，迫于公剧，末暇视尔 - Literal translation: 'This year, I was appointed by God as the judge in the underworld, and I am too busy with official duties to see you.' '司宪' means judge, and '公剧' refers to official duties.
  • 郑氏偶为避难，移家阳曲 - Literal translation: 'The Zheng family has temporarily moved to Yangqu to avoid danger.' '避难' means to seek refuge.
  • 新妇贤淑，终当合并 - Literal translation: 'Your future wife is virtuous and beautiful, and you will eventually be united.' '合并' suggests a reunion or marriage.
  • 吾已使人筹画，尔无忧也 - Literal translation: 'I have already arranged things, so you have nothing to worry about.' '筹画' means to plan or arrange.
  • 尔师厚德，吾所深铭 - Literal translation: 'Your teacher's great virtue is deeply etched in my memory.' '厚德' means great virtue.
  • 玉前奉尔师金帛，皆吾俸馀之物 - Literal translation: 'I previously gave your teacher gold and silk, which were all from my salary.' '俸馀之物' means salary surplus.
  • 为吾谢之，匪以云报，亦故人地下之情耳 - Literal translation: 'Please convey my thanks to him; it is not a return for his kindness, but a gesture of friendship from the afterlife.' '匪以云报' means not as a repayment.
  • 尔母吾不及晤，尔其告之 - Literal translation: 'I did not have the chance to meet your mother; please tell her.' '晤' means to meet.
  • 露觉而异之，闻者亦莫不嗟叹 - Literal translation: 'Lu woke up and found it strange; those who heard about it also could not help but sigh.' '嗟叹' means to sigh or lament.
  • 然“合并”之语，莫得而明，意谓非死不能也，露殆不永矣，反更忧之 - Literal translation: 'However, the meaning of 'merger' is unclear; it seems to imply that it can only happen after death. Lu may not live long, and instead, he became even more worried.' '合并' suggests a reunion or marriage, and '殆不永矣' means he may not live long.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 甚矣，鬼之难识也 - An exclamatory phrase meaning 'How difficult it is to recognize ghosts!' The structure '甚矣, ... 也' is used to emphasize the difficulty or complexity of something.
  • 当二人疑惧之际，彼此互观，觉衣服、手足、耳目事事皆鬼，而实则非鬼 - Describes a situation where two people, filled with doubt and fear, look at each other and see everything about each other as ghostly, even though they are not actually ghosts. '事事皆鬼' means 'everything seems like a ghost.'
  • 而为之居间而排难者乃真鬼，而反亲之而求助也 - Indicates that the one who acts as a mediator and resolves the difficulty is actually a ghost, and they are trusted and helped by the two people. '居间而排难' means 'to mediate and resolve difficulties.'
  • 使鬼不乘衅而出，或见于二人独行之时，则毒手饱拳，鬼当之矣 - A conditional statement suggesting that if the ghost did not take advantage of the situation to appear, they might have appeared when the two people were alone, and the ghost would have attacked them with '毒手饱拳' (poisonous hands and full fists). '乘衅而出' means 'to take advantage of the opportunity to appear.'
  • 此鬼之所熟筹而万万不出于此者也 - This phrase means 'This is something the ghost has thoroughly considered and would never do.' '熟筹' means 'thoroughly considered,' and '万万不出于此' means 'would never do this.'
  • 呜呼！鬼亦狡谲矣哉 - An exclamatory phrase expressing pity or surprise, meaning 'Alas! How cunning the ghost is!'

  • 大王 - A title for a powerful or authoritative figure, often used in feudal contexts.
  • 石室 - A stone chamber, often associated with ancient tombs or hidden places in Chinese literature.
  • 虎头人 - A person with a tiger's head, a supernatural being in Chinese mythology.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 越数年 - Indicates a passage of time, common in narrative texts.
  • 其人 - Refers to a person, likely a friend or acquaintance.
  • 余 - Refers to the narrator, likely the author himself.
  • 阍者 - Refers to a gatekeeper or servant, indicating a household setting.
  • 某氏 - Refers to a person by their family name, a common practice in Classical Chinese to avoid naming individuals directly.
  • 神验 - Indicates divine accuracy, often associated with divination or prophecy.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '越数年，其人来访。余辞以他出，以观其能。' must be read as a narrative progression: 'After several years, the person came to visit. I excused myself by saying I was out, to see his ability.'
  • The dialogue '其人笑曰：“正在枣树下观书，何诳也”' must be read as a direct quote, with the rhetorical question expressing disbelief.
  • The sequence '阍者惊报。相见大笑，盘桓而去。' must be read as a series of actions: 'The gatekeeper was surprised and reported. We met and laughed, staying for a while and then left.'
  • The sentence '余晚年术益进，家居闭户，不肯衒于人。' must be read as a self-referential statement: 'In my old age, my skills advanced further, and I stayed at home, not flaunting it to people.'
  • The sentence '间露数事，皆神验。' must be read as a summary of selective disclosures: 'Occasionally, I revealed a few things, all of which were divinely accurate.'
  • The sequence '有村人修屋，问当以何日毕工。余期以某日某时，且属勿后。' must be read as a narrative progression: 'A villager was building a house and asked when it would be completed. I predicted a specific date and time, and instructed him not to be late.'
  • The sequence '及期工毕，大雨踵至，淋漓旬日。' must be read as a narrative progression: 'When the predicted date arrived, the work was completed, and heavy rain followed, lasting for ten days.'
  • The sequence '又尝薄暮游某氏园，见瓜棚上瓜甚夥，向某氏求二枚。' must be read as a narrative progression: 'Once, late in the afternoon, I visited Mr. X's garden and saw many melons on the trellis. I asked Mr. X for two.'
  • The sequence '某氏许之。余请自携去，某氏曰：“公老人，焉能挟此重物明日当遣人送至。”' must be read as a dialogue: 'Mr. X agreed. I asked to carry them myself, and Mr. X said, 'You are an old man, how could you carry such a heavy load? I will send someone to deliver them tomorrow.'
  • The sequence '余曰：“若是，则空言矣。”某氏笑曰：“公岂疑我食言耶”' must be read as a dialogue: 'I said, 'If so, it would be empty words.' Mr. X laughed and said, 'Do you doubt that I would go back on my word?'
  • The sequence '是夜，某氏瓜为偷儿盗尽，始悟余请自携之故，盖预知之也。' must be read as a narrative progression: 'That night, the melons of Mr. X were stolen by thieves. I realized the reason I asked to carry them myself, as I had foreseen it.'

  • 某氏 - A person by their family name, likely a villager or acquaintance.

  • 皆落花砌筑，久而胶结，固于金石 - 'All built from fallen petals, over time they have bonded, more solid than metal and stone,' a metaphorical description of the city's construction.
  • 谓天下之春皆从此出 - 'It is said that all the spring in the world comes from here,' a poetic and symbolic statement.
  • 城内辉煌烂漫，不可目穷 - 'Inside the city, it was brilliant and colorful, too much to see at once,' describing the overwhelming beauty.
  • 皆四时之花同时开放 - 'All the flowers of the four seasons bloomed at the same time,' indicating the extraordinary nature of the place.
  • 颜色相间，掩映而蔽亏也 - 'Colors intermingled, overlapping and partially hidden,' describing the intricate and beautiful arrangement of the flowers.
  • 行万花中，径甚缭曲 - 'Walking among the myriad flowers, the path was very winding,' indicating the complexity and beauty of the garden.
  • 觉神明爽发，肤髪皆香 - 'Feeling refreshed and invigorated, his skin and hair were perfumed,' describing the sensory experience.
  • 有顷而至 - 'After a while, they arrived,' indicating the passage of time.
  • 宫阙亭台，朱甍碧瓦，鳞次而带续也 - 'Palaces, pavilions, red eaves, and green tiles, arranged in a fish-scale pattern and connected,' describing the grandeur and beauty of the architecture.
  • 阍者皆韶艳女郎 - 'The gatekeepers were all beautiful young women,' indicating the presence of attractive attendants.
  • 驰报夫人 - 'Rushed to report to Madam,' showing the urgency of the message.
  • 元降舆以待 - 'Xuan stepped down from the carriage to wait,' indicating his readiness to meet the lady.
  • 红英翠叶之间 - 'Among the red blossoms and green leaves,' describing the setting.
  • 飘轻裙、曳广袖 - 'Dancing in light skirts and wide sleeves,' describing the graceful movements of the flower goddesses.
  • 美丽闲都者无虑千百 - 'Beautiful and elegant, numbering in the hundreds,' indicating the large number of attendants.
  • 私语匿笑，往来窥客 - 'Whispering and giggling, they peeked at the guests,' describing the playful and curious behavior of the attendants.
  • 以问 - 'Xuan asked,' indicating his curiosity.
  • 花神也 - 'They are the flower goddesses,' revealing the identity of the attendants.
  • 花一种，即有一神 - 'Each type of flower has its own goddess,' explaining the diversity of the attendants.
  • 丰约秾纤不一 - 'Their figures vary in fullness and slimness,' describing the diverse appearances of the goddesses.
  • 花亦如之 - 'The flowers are also varied,' reinforcing the metaphor of the goddesses and flowers.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 长春苑 - 'Garden of Eternal Spring,' a mythical place often associated with immortality and eternal youth in Chinese literature.
  • 夫人 - 'Madam,' a title often used for noblewomen or important figures.
  • 花神 - 'Flower goddesses,' supernatural beings associated with flowers and the changing seasons in Chinese folklore.
  • 四时之花 - 'Flowers of the four seasons,' referring to the variety of flowers that bloom throughout the year, symbolizing the cyclical nature of life and nature.

Proper Nouns

  • 长春苑 - 'Garden of Eternal Spring,' a mythical place.
  • 夫人 - 'Madam,' a title for a noblewoman or important figure.
  • 花神 - 'Flower goddesses,' supernatural beings associated with flowers and the changing seasons.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 已而 - Classical adverb meaning 'after a while' or 'soon after,' indicating a passage of time.
  • 阍者 - Refers to a gatekeeper or porter, a common figure in classical Chinese narrative.
  • 传夫人请见 - Formal expression meaning 'the gatekeeper conveyed the lady's request to see [someone].'
  • 元肃容而入 - 'Yuan entered with a respectful demeanor,' indicating formal or reverent behavior.
  • 历门闼十数重 - Phrase meaning 'passed through ten or more gates,' emphasizing the grandeur and complexity of the setting.
  • 珠帘乍卷，翟葆斜开 - Poetic description of the opening of a curtain of pearls and the unfolding of a ceremonial train (翟葆), evoking a majestic and ethereal scene.
  • 嫔从如云，左右环簇 - Metaphor meaning 'the attendants were like clouds, surrounding from all sides,' emphasizing the grandeur and density of the retinue.
  • 冠华玉之冠 - 'Wore a crown adorned with jade and flowers,' describing an ornate headdress.
  • 履彩云之舄 - 'Wore shoes inlaid with patterns of clouds,' a metaphor for elaborate footwear.
  • 衣裳霞艳 - 'Her attire was as radiant as the morning clouds,' a poetic description of her clothing.
  • 环佩鸾鸣 - Metaphor for the sound of jade ornaments and bells, likened to the cry of a phoenix, symbolizing elegance and grace.
  • 神影仙姿 - 'Divine image and fairy-like form,' a poetic expression of supernatural beauty.
  • 惊绝人世 - 'Surpassing the world in amazement,' indicating an extraordinary and breathtaking appearance.
  • 降阶而迎 - 'Descended the steps to greet,' a gesture of honor and welcome.
  • 元下拜 - 'Yuan knelt and bowed,' a formal act of respect.
  • 夫人命侍女曳起，赐坐 - 'The lady ordered a maid to help him up and granted him a seat,' indicating a gesture of grace and hospitality.
  • 夫人南向，元东向 - Classical seating arrangement indicating the relative status of the participants. In ancient Chinese, the south-facing position was the most honorable.
  • 秀才以惜花之心，成爱花之癖 - 'The scholar, with a heart devoted to cherishing flowers, has developed a passion for them,' indicating a cultivated and refined interest.
  • 盖奇绿也 - 'This is a rare and unusual act,' with 奇绿 possibly being a rare or unique event (possibly a typo or variant of 奇遇 or 奇观).
  • 逊谢 - 'Humbly declined or expressed gratitude,' a polite response.
  • 俄而设享 - 'Soon after, the feast was set up,' indicating the transition from ceremony to banquet.
  • 绮席相次 - 'Silken seats arranged in succession,' describing the luxurious seating.
  • 凡诸花神，悉令陪宴 - 'All the flower deities were invited to join the banquet,' showing the supernatural and mythological elements of the scene.
  • 进飞英红露之酿 - 'Served a drink made from the nectar of blooming flowers and red dew,' a poetic description of an elixir or wine.
  • 陈词香和玉之羞 - 'Served delicacies that smelled of fragrance and were as pure as jade,' describing the food as both aromatic and refined.
  • 酒肴数周 - 'The feast lasted for several rounds,' indicating the length of the banquet.
  • 众乐并作 - 'All the music played together,' suggesting a full and harmonious performance.
  • 间歌叠舞 - 'Songs and dances alternated,' describing the performance style.
  • 遏雪停云 - Poetic expression suggesting the music was so powerful it seemed to stop the falling snow and drifting clouds.
  • 风调各殊，容华并绝 - 'Each had its own style and beauty, all of them extraordinary,' describing the diversity and excellence of the performances.
  • 惜弄玉、飞琼请姊妹方宴瑶池 - 'Regret that Lady Nongyu and Lady Feiqiong, who were sisters, were feasting in the Jade Pool of the immortal realm,' referencing mythological figures associated with flowers and beauty.
  • 来暇邀致，使今日管弦未臻极盛 - 'They had no leisure to invite them, so today's music and instruments have not reached their peak,' expressing a sense of incompleteness.
  • 元捧觞避席 - 'Yuan raised the cup and stepped aside from his seat,' a gesture of respect and humility.
  • 躬际斯盛 - 'To personally witness such a grand event,' indicating the honor of being present.
  • 皆夫人辱命之宠也 - 'All of this is due to the lady's gracious command,' expressing gratitude.
  • 愿借芳尊，为夫人寿 - 'I wish to raise this fragrant cup to toast the lady's longevity,' a traditional gesture of respect.
  • 夫人起而受之，亦酌酒酬元 - 'The lady rose to accept the toast and also poured a drink to honor Yuan,' showing mutual respect and reciprocity.
  • 日昃罢宴 - 'The feast ended at the time of the sun's decline,' indicating the passage of time.
  • 以笙歌羽卫，送居丛芳馆 - 'With music, singing, and retinue, they escorted him to the Pavilion of Fragrant Flowers,' describing the ceremonial departure.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 元 - Likely a name, possibly a scholar or literary figure, though not a historically known figure.
  • 夫人 - Refers to a noblewoman or deity, possibly a goddess of flowers or the Queen of the Flower Realm.
  • 翟葆 - A ceremonial train or robe worn by high-ranking women, often associated with imperial or divine figures.
  • 花神 - 'Flower deities' or 'deities of the flowers,' a common motif in Chinese mythology and poetry, symbolizing beauty, nature, and the seasons.
  • 弄玉、飞琼 - Mythological figures from Chinese legend, often associated with the Queen of the Flower Realm or the immortal realm. 弄玉 (Nongyu) and 飞琼 (Feiqiong) are often paired as sisters or companions in the immortal court.
  • 瑶池 - The 'Jade Pool' of the Queen Mother of Heaven (Xi Wangmu), a mythical location in Chinese cosmology where immortals gather.
  • 丛芳馆 - 'Pavilion of Fragrant Flowers,' a poetic name for a garden or retreat associated with flowers, possibly a fictional or symbolic location.

Proper Nouns

  • 元 - A name, possibly a scholar or literary figure. No specific historical identity is known.
  • 夫人 - A title, likely a noblewoman or deity, not a specific historical person.
  • 弄玉 - Mythological figure, often associated with the Queen of the Flower Realm or the immortal court.
  • 飞琼 - Mythological figure, often associated with the Queen of the Flower Realm or the immortal court.
  • 瑶池 - The 'Jade Pool' of the Queen Mother of Heaven, a mythological location.
  • 丛芳馆 - 'Pavilion of Fragrant Flowers,' a symbolic or fictional location.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 明日令下 - Means 'the next day, the order was issued.'
  • 以元为长春苑催花使者 - Means 'appointed Yuan as the envoy to the Changchun Garden to hasten the blooming of flowers.'
  • 并赐青幡绛节，彩仗云骈 - Means 'and granted him a green banner, a red scepter, and colorful flags in a cloud-like formation.'
  • 披服宝玉 - Means 'wearing precious jade ornaments.'
  • 出入府第 - Means 'coming and going in and out of the mansion.'
  • 花神闻之，皆来贺 - Means 'when the flower spirits heard of it, they all came to congratulate.'
  • 夫人命侍儿送花名玉册至 - Means 'the lady-in-waiting was ordered to bring the jade-bound register of flower names.'
  • 元阅其姓字，皆往古美人 - Means 'Yuan read their names, and they were all ancient beauties.'
  • 乃悟花神即美人也 - Means 'and realized that the flower spirits were the same as the ancient beauties.'
  • 出世为美人，离世为花神 - Means 'they became beauties in this world and flower spirits after leaving it.'
  • 元欲识其尤者 - Means 'Yuan wanted to recognize the most outstanding among them.'
  • 因谓众美曰 - Means 'so he said to the beautiful ones.'
  • 诸卿芳名，心写久矣 - Means 'your noble names have been long cherished in my heart.'
  • 今鄙人愿有以观焉，敢请 - Means 'now, I humbly wish to see you, if I may ask.'
  • 一女迟回而出，意度闲稚 - Means 'a woman hesitated and then emerged, with a leisurely and innocent demeanor.'
  • 元揖谓之曰 - Means 'Yuan bowed and said to her.'
  • 卿织锦璇玑图，八百四十字，宛转循环，有诗三千余首，古今才妙莫或右之 - Means 'You wove the *Xuanji Tu* (璇玑图) with 840 characters, which is intricately interwoven and contains over 3,000 poems, unmatched in talent and skill throughout history.'
  • 若兰逊谢 - Means 'Su Ruolan modestly declined the praise.'
  • 次问红拂 - Means 'Next, he asked about Hong Fu.'
  • 元视其貌，修然绝尘 - Means 'Yuan looked at her appearance, which was elegant and transcendent.'
  • 识药师于晋谒，结虬髯于逆旅 - Means 'She recognized the Buddhist master during his visit and befriended the man with the curly beard in an inn.'
  • 红妆俊眼，真希有也 - Means 'Her beauty and sharp eyes are truly rare.'
  • 红拂退，元目送久之 - Means 'Hong Fu stepped back, and Yuan watched her for a long time.'
  • 忽见一女于稠人中俯而摩其足，若不胜其楚者 - Means 'Suddenly, he saw a woman in the crowd who was bending down and rubbing her foot, as if in pain.'
  • 以纤月之勾，妙凌云之舞，真不让掌上人矣 - Means 'With her slender waist like a crescent moon and her graceful dance that soars to the clouds, she is truly no less than a dancer on the palm of a hand.'
  • 窅颊赤 - Means 'Yan Niang blushed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 长春苑 - Refers to the Changchun Garden, a royal garden in Chinese history.
  • 催花使者 - Refers to an official or envoy tasked with hastening the blooming of flowers, often associated with seasonal festivals or rituals.
  • 青幡绛节 - Refers to ceremonial banners and scepters, symbols of authority and honor.
  • 彩仗云骈 - Refers to a colorful procession or parade, often used in imperial or ceremonial contexts.
  • 披服宝玉 - Refers to wearing precious jade ornaments, a symbol of wealth and status.
  • 花神 - Refers to flower spirits, mythical beings associated with flowers in Chinese folklore.
  • 往古美人 - Refers to ancient beauties, often idealized figures in Chinese literature and art.
  • 苏若兰 - Refers to Su Ruolan, a legendary figure known for her literary talents, particularly for the *Xuanji Tu* (璇玑图).
  • 红拂 - Refers to Hong Fu, a famous courtesan and warrior in Chinese literature, known for her intelligence and beauty.
  • 药师 - Refers to a Buddhist master, likely a reference to a historical or legendary figure.
  • 虬髯 - Refers to a man with curly or bushy beard, often associated with the legendary hero Li Shimin (Emperor Taizong of Tang).
  • 窅娘 - Refers to Yan Niang, a famous dancer in Chinese history, known for her graceful and acrobatic performances.

Proper Nouns

  • 长春苑 - The Changchun Garden.
  • 催花使者 - The envoy to hasten the blooming of flowers.

  • 燕兒燕兒，又作昭陽故本矣 - A sarcastic remark implying that the woman is repeating her past behavior, possibly referring to the historical figure Zhao Feiyan (飛燕).
  • 何與爾梅精事 - A rhetorical question expressing disbelief or dismissal of the other woman's involvement.
  • 楊妃亦謂樓東寂寥 - A phrase referring to Yang Guifei (楊妃) feeling lonely in the east wing of the palace.
  • 奈何以舌鋒刺人 - A phrase expressing frustration at being verbally attacked.
  • 珍珠可慰 - A suggestion that Pearl (珍珠) could provide comfort.
  • 不記環上系羅衣時耶 - A sarcastic reminder of a past event, possibly referring to a time when they were close or involved in similar situations.
  • 元問之 - A phrase referring to Yuan (元), likely a mediator or observer.
  • 乃班婕妤也 - A phrase identifying the speaker as Ban Jieyu (班婕妤), a historical figure known for her poetry and grace.
  • 恐其攻軋無已 - A phrase expressing concern that the conflict might continue indefinitely.
  • 亟為解之 - A phrase urging Yuan to intervene and resolve the situation.
  • 譬諸花枝糾結，花片相撲 - A metaphor comparing the conflict to entangled branches and petals colliding, suggesting that it is ultimately harmless but regrettable.
  • 徒令人可憐耳 - A phrase concluding that the situation is pitiable but not harmful.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 他日 - Temporal phrase meaning 'on another day' or 'later on.'
  • 夫人命元定花神甲乙 - Means 'the lady ordered Yuan to rank the flower deities from first to last.'
  • 元辞让再三，不获 - Yuan repeatedly declined but was not successful in his refusal.
  • 甄别其香，艳 - '甄别' means to distinguish or classify, and '香' and '艳' refer to fragrance and beauty respectively.
  • 各为三品 - Means 'each into three grades.'
  • 香曰奇香、名香、幽香 - Fragrance is divided into three grades: peculiar fragrance, famous fragrance, and subtle fragrance.
  • 艳曰雅艳、秾艳、狂艳 - Beauty is divided into three grades: elegant beauty, rich beauty, and wild beauty.
  • 视其神以定品，而花隶焉 - Means 'to determine their grade based on their spirit, and the flowers follow suit.'
  • 如西施、王嫱、卓文君、崔双文之属 - Refers to specific historical or legendary figures known for their beauty.
  • 咸列狂艳 - All are listed under the category of wild beauty.
  • 妾虽鄙陋，君何至以狂艳见目 - Means 'Although I am humble and unremarkable, why would you classify me as wild beauty?'
  • 固当小贬 - Means 'should be slightly downgraded.'
  • 沼吴之后，妾实从伍相于江流 - After the fall of Wu, I actually followed Wu Xiang in the river.
  • 陶朱何人，妾宁俪之，以负君恩而丧妇节也 - '陶朱' refers to Fan Li, a famous statesman and merchant. The phrase means 'Who is Fan Li? I would never associate with him, for it would betray your kindness and my virtue.'
  • 微卿言，吾几误矣 - Means 'Without your words, I almost made a mistake.'
  • 远嫁沙漠 - Means 'married far away to the desert.'
  • 不敢违，未尝一日忘汉也 - Means 'I dared not disobey, and I have never forgotten Han for a single day.'
  • 佞臣秉笔，诬以聚麀之行 - '佞臣' refers to a sycophantic minister, and '聚麀之行' means 'having relations with multiple partners.'
  • 饮恨黄沙，末由昭雪 - Means 'to drink bitterness in the yellow sand, without a chance to clear one's name.'
  • 冢草独青 - Means 'the grave grass remains uniquely green.'
  • 僧孺《周秦行记》乃敢肄为狂言 - '僧孺' refers to Bai Xingjian, and '《周秦行记》' is his work. The phrase means 'Bai Xingjian in his *Journey Through the Zhou and Qin Dynasties* dared to speak wildly.'
  • 深相污蔑，此妾之所痛心而茹愤者也 - Means 'deeply defaming me, which deeply pains me and fills me with resentment.'
  • 君其察之 - Means 'please investigate this.'
  • 乃列二人于幽香，始悦而去 - Means 'then listed the two under subtle fragrance, and they were pleased and left.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 元以久别山居 - 元 is a common surname; 久别山居 means long-separated from the mountain retreat, referring to the author's desire to return home.
  • 欲乞假暂归 - 欲乞假 means wants to request leave; 暂归 means to return temporarily.
  • 窃惟芳草相思 - 窃惟 is a humble way of saying I think; 芳草 refers to fragrant grass; 相思 means longing or missing someone.
  • 王孙惆帐 - 王孙 refers to a noble youth; 惆帐 means sad or melancholy.
  • 垂杨惹恨 - 垂杨 refers to willow trees; 惹恨 means causing resentment or stirring up sorrow.
  • 游子踯踌 - 游子 means wanderer or traveler; 踯踌 means hesitating or pausing.
  • 富贵有乡国之思 - 富贵 means wealth and honor; 乡国 refers to one's hometown or country; 思 means thought or longing.
  • 神仙多别离之感 - 神仙 refers to immortals or deities; 别离之感 means the feeling of separation.
  • 臣本蒭荛 - 臣 means I or subject; 本 means originally; 蒭荛 refers to a common person or a farmer.
  • 辱在草莽 - 辱 means dishonor; 草莽 refers to the wilderness or rural areas.
  • 芝无田而蕙无囿 - 芝 and 蕙 are types of fragrant herbs; 无田 means no fields; 无囿 means no gardens.
  • 税驾何乡 - 税驾 means to rest one's carriage; 何乡 means which place.
  • 岸有芷而汀有兰 - 岸 means shore; 芷 is a type of fragrant herb; 汀 means sandbank; 兰 is another type of fragrant herb.
  • 寻芳迷路 - 寻芳 means to seek fragrance or to look for beauty; 迷路 means to get lost.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有畫師客其家 - Literal: 'There was a painter who was a guest in his house.' Implies a painter who visited or stayed as a guest.
  • 自言有奇畫 - 'Spoke of having a strange/surprising painting.' '奇' can mean strange, unusual, or extraordinary.
  • 願与众观之 - 'Wished to show it to the crowd.' '众' is a collective term for 'the people' or 'the group.'
  • 乃张画于壁 - 'Then he hung the painting on the wall.' '张' is a classical term for hanging or displaying.
  • 类西洋画 - 'Resembled Western-style painting.' '类' means 'resemble' or 'similar to.'
  • 徐问画有何奇 - 'Xu asked what was strange about the painting.' '徐' is a name; the structure is 'Xu asked what was strange about the painting.'
  • 吾能身入其中，故奇也 - 'I can enter it myself, hence it is strange.' '身入其中' is a literal expression meaning 'enter into it bodily.'
  • 帖然一纸 - 'A flat sheet of paper.' '帖然' means flat or smooth.
  • 涂以丹碧 - 'Painted with red and blue.' '丹' is red, '碧' is green or blue.
  • 公乌能置身其间乎 - 'How can you place yourself inside it?' '乌' is an archaic particle used for rhetorical questions, often meaning 'how' or 'why.'
  • 门亦随合 - 'The door closed behind him as well.' '随合' means 'closed with it' or 'followed suit.'
  • 扪之如故 - 'Touching it felt the same as before.' '扪' is to touch or feel.
  • 索之不可得也 - 'They searched for it but could not find it.' '索' is to search or seek.
  • 共相惊怪 - 'They all were surprised and amazed.' '惊怪' is a compound meaning 'to be surprised and puzzled.'
  • 门复启，而画师瞥下矣 - 'The door opened again, and the painter descended.' '瞥下' is a bit ambiguous; likely means 'stepped down' or 'descended.'
  • 何问为第同往观之 - 'Why ask? Just come and see it together.' '何问为' is a rhetorical question meaning 'why ask?' or 'what need is there to ask?'
  • 当令开此正户，以迓诸君 - 'I will have this main gate opened to welcome you all.' '迓' is to welcome or receive.
  • 户应手而开 - 'The gate opened at the touch of the hand.' '应手' means 'responded to the hand' or 'opened at a touch.'
  • 一一以手捷之入 - 'He led them in one by one with his hand.' '捷' is a rare character, likely meaning 'to guide' or 'to lead.'
  • 鸟革翠飞 - 'Bird feathers and green flying.' Likely a poetic description of decoration or color.
  • 埒于王侯甲第 - 'Comparable to the mansions of princes and nobles.' '埒' means equal or comparable; '甲第' is the top-tier mansions or noble estates.
  • 每历一门闼，便觉改观 - 'Each time they passed through a gate, the scene changed.' '历' is to pass through; '门闼' is gate or portal.
  • 或粉壁森然，忽启双摩 - 'Sometimes a white wall stood tall, then suddenly two doors opened.' '双摩' is unclear; possibly '双门' (two doors) or a poetic term for doors.
  • 或画栋巍然，忽开叠阁 - 'Sometimes painted beams stood towering, then suddenly a tiered pavilion opened.' '叠阁' is a layered pavilion or multi-level structure.
  • 或窗间缒下，别有亭池 - 'Sometimes from a window, a rope was let down, revealing a pavilion and pond.' '缒下' is to lower something with a rope.
  • 或石罅穿入，另开园圃 - 'Sometimes through a crack in the stone, another garden was revealed.' '石罅' is a fissure or crack in stone.
  • 其堂室之形制，矩方之外，有似月者，有似圭者，有似弓者，有似扇者，有似蕉叶者，有似香炉者，有似钟者，有似环玦者，有似壶瓮各器者 - 'The shape of the hall and rooms, beyond the square, resembled the moon, the jade tablet, the bow, the fan, the banana leaf, the incense burner, the bell, the ring, and various vessels.' This is a list of shapes using similes.
  • 瑰形诡制，无一雷同 - 'Marvelous forms and strange structures, none of which were alike.' '瑰形' is beautiful or strange shapes; '诡制' is strange designs or structures; '雷同' is to be the same or alike.

  • 令曰：“不然。心者身之宰也，心变故形变，即形变而心不宜变。且真虎杀人亦法所不贷，况女而虎其父母者欤” - The county official said, 'Not so. The heart is the master of the body, if the heart changes, then the form changes, even if the form changes, the heart should not change. Even if a real tiger kills someone, the law will not spare it, how much more so if a girl turns into a tiger and kills her parents.'
  • 非非子曰：令之言然哉！夫虎，猛者也，亦灵者也。人而虎也，宜不猛面更灵君子于是知女之处心积虑而后成于虎，不然，何昧于亲而辨于婿也荆人之死于虎欤荆人之死于女也。而牛哀之变又不足言也，噎！ - The commentator said, 'The county official's words are right! A tiger is fierce and also intelligent. If a person turns into a tiger, they should not be fierce but more intelligent. Gentlemen understand that the girl had been thinking and planning and turned into a tiger, otherwise, why would she not recognize her parents but recognize her husband. Did the people of Jing die at the hands of the tiger or the girl. And the transformation of Niu Ai is not worth mentioning, sigh.'

  • 杀其子竟绝其嗣矣 - The sentence structure is compact, and the meaning can be interpreted as "killing the son will ultimately cut off his descendants."
  • 于此见天之妙用，于此见雷之苦心 - The sentence structure is parallel, and the meaning can be interpreted as "one can see the divine wisdom and the thunder god's earnest intentions in this situation."
  • 痘神何神也姑勿深考 - The sentence structure is elliptical, and the meaning can be interpreted as "the deity of smallpox, let's not delve too deeply into it."
  • 言语稍不检，衣物稍不洁，及诚敬少懈者，病者辄作神言语呵谴之 - The sentence structure is complex, and the meaning can be interpreted as "if the patient is slightly careless in speech, slightly unclean in clothing, or slightly lacking in sincerity and reverence, the deity will speak through the patient and rebuke them."
  • 灵界之迹不可胜纪，然亦非妄祸人者 - The sentence structure is complex, and the meaning can be interpreted as "the traces of the deity are too numerous to recount, but they are not one who causes harm without reason."

  • 不闻哭声 - Literal translation: 'Did not hear crying sounds.' In context, suggests absence of mourning or distress.
  • 窃窃私语 - Whispering secretly. Refers to hushed, furtive conversation, often associated with secrecy or fear.
  • 伺门外 - Waiting outside the door. Implies vigilance or surveillance.
  • 若谋机事恐人觉者 - As if plotting a secret matter, fearing someone might discover them. Suggests clandestine activity.
  • 完古甚怪 - Wan Gu was greatly surprised. A phrase expressing strong surprise or confusion.
  • 是何哭声之多也 - Why is there so much crying? A rhetorical question expressing bewilderment.
  • 男女乍见，皆惊避 - The men and women, upon seeing him, were startled and fled. Suggests fear or surprise at the unexpected visitor.
  • 客也，何避为 - He is a guest, why are you hiding? A question expressing confusion or accusation.
  • 衣履甚敝 - His clothes and shoes were very worn. Describes poverty or hardship.
  • 椎鲁类农夫 - Stolid and dull, like a farmer. Describes someone simple-minded or unrefined, likely of the lower class.
  • 自丞簿以上贪残如狼虎 - From the county magistrate down to the clerks, they are as greedy and cruel as wolves and tigers. A metaphor for extreme corruption.
  • 工役繁兴 - Many labor projects are being carried out. Refers to excessive state labor demands.
  • 科派乡里 - Imposing taxes on the village. A form of forced taxation.
  • 日役丁男敷千 - Daily, thousands of able-bodied men are conscripted. '敷千' is a classical expression meaning 'thousands' or 'many thousands.'
  • 少给其食 - Given little food. Indicates harsh treatment of laborers.
  • 小民失业，劳苦吁天 - The common people are unemployed, suffering greatly and crying out to heaven. A metaphor for despair and injustice.
  • 饥馑洊臻 - Famine occurs repeatedly. '洊臻' means 'repeatedly arriving' or 'occurring in succession.'
  • 道殣相望 - Along the roads, corpses are seen. Describes the grim reality of starvation and death.
  • 无以供租税 - Unable to pay taxes. Indicates economic collapse.
  • 悍吏日来吾乡，叫嚣隳突，鸡狗不宁 - Brutal officials come daily to our village, shouting and causing chaos; even the chickens and dogs are not at peace. A vivid description of oppression.
  • 乡民流离，死丧殆尽 - The villagers have been scattered, and nearly all have died. Indicates near-total depopulation.
  • 副使且至矣 - The deputy governor is about to arrive. Refers to an imminent official visit or inspection.
  • 县官捉人除道 - The county officials are capturing people to clear the roads. Implies forced labor for road construction.
  • 副使爪牙千百，所在骚扰 - The deputy governor has thousands of henchmen, who cause disturbance everywhere. A metaphor for the oppressive power of the deputy governor.
  • 不死于劳悴，则死于敲扑 - They do not die from exhaustion, but from beatings. Highlights the brutality of the system.
  • 仓卒之间，未能远徒，且谋宵遁耳 - In such a sudden situation, we cannot flee far, so we are planning to escape at night. Indicates desperation and last-minute plans.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 完古 - A name, likely a fictional or historical figure. No known historical reference, so treated as a proper name.
  • 丞簿 - Refers to lower-ranking county officials. In the Han and later dynasties, the county magistrate (*xian guan*) was the highest local official, and below him were clerks and assistants (*cheng bu*), who handled administrative tasks.
  • 工役繁兴 - Refers to the heavy labor demands of the state, often seen during periods of large-scale construction or military campaigns.
  • 科派乡里 - A common practice in feudal China where local officials imposed taxes or labor obligations on the village.
  • 丁男 - 'Able-bodied men,' typically adult males conscripted for labor or military service.
  • 饥馑洊臻 - Describes a recurring or prolonged period of famine, which was a frequent issue in pre-modern China due to war, corruption, or natural disaster.
  • 道殣相望 - A vivid image from classical literature, often used to depict the devastation of war or famine.
  • 悍吏 - 'Brutal officials' or corrupt local magistrates who abused their power.
  • 副使 - A deputy governor or high-ranking official sent by the central government to inspect or oversee local administration.
  • 县官捉人除道 - Implies the use of forced labor for road construction, a common practice in imperial China.
  • 敲扑 - Refers to corporal punishment using sticks or whips. '敲' is to beat, '扑' is to strike.
  • 宵遁 - 'To flee at night.' A term used in classical texts to describe secret or desperate escape.

Proper Nouns

  • 完古 - A name; likely a fictional character or historical figure. Rendered as 'Wan Gu' consistently.
  • 副使 - 'Deputy governor' or 'deputy official.' Consistently rendered as 'deputy governor.'
  • 丞簿 - A collective term for lower-ranking county officials. Rendered as 'county clerks and assistants' or 'lower-ranking county officials.'
  • 县官 - 'County official' or 'county magistrate.' Consistently rendered as 'county officials.'
  • 乡里 - Village or local community. Refers to the rural population under the authority of county officials.

  • 两人之怨自此始 - Their relationship begins to deteriorate from this point.
  • 玉每于帘下窥生，动至永日，生亦为之神魂寓舍 - Yu Zanzan secretly watches Lu Sheng through the curtains, often for long periods, Lu Sheng is captivated.
  • 屡托媒者致辞，而余氏以生困弱，坚不许 - Lu Sheng repeatedly asks matchmakers to propose marriage, Yu Zanzan's family refuses due to his poverty and weakness.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • Long sequence describing a sudden and rapid appearance of a creature, emphasizing its unusual size and lack of seven apertures.
  • Sequence describing the reaction of three objects to the creature, emphasizing the speed and sudden disappearance of the objects.
  • Phrase concluding the description of the creature, expressing the mystery surrounding it.
  • Sequence introducing Lu Sheng, providing background information about his character.
  • Sequence describing the relationship between Lu Sheng and Yu Zanzan, emphasizing their close bond and exceptional qualities.
  • Sequence describing the reaction of Yu Zanzan's mother to the relationship between Lu Sheng and Yu Zanzan, and how they understood and became even more devoted to each other.
  • Sequence describing the development of their relationship through poetry, emphasizing their affectionate exchanges.
  • Sequence describing the turning point in their relationship, where Yu Zanzan's father forbids Lu Sheng from visiting her home, leading to their estrangement.
  • Sequence describing the secret watching and mutual captivation between Yu Zanzan and Lu Sheng.
  • Sequence describing Lu Sheng's repeated attempts to propose marriage and Yu Zanzan's family's refusal.

  • 莘昭 - A name or a reference to a historical or literary figure. *莘* is a surname, and *昭* can mean 'bright' or 'clear.'
  • 勢異松蘿 - A metaphor from classical poetry, where *松* (pine) and *蘿* (vine) are often used to represent two entities that once supported each other but are now separated.
  • 嫌防瓜李 - A reference to the idiom *瓜李之嫌*, which alludes to the suspicion or scandal of a romantic relationship between a man and a woman.
  • 精衛填東海之波 - A reference to the myth of Jingwei, who tried to fill the East Sea with stones to avenge her drowning sister. This is a symbol of persistent effort or futile struggle.

  • 独坐场隅三日，而众莫之识 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'sitting alone in a corner for three days, and no one recognized him,' indicating the Taoist's isolation.

  • 道人以杖叩石壁，石即洞开 - Narrative phrase meaning 'the Taoist knocked on the stone wall with his staff, and the stone opened,' describing the supernatural event.

  • 至前庭，窥其内昏黑不辨物，徘徊不敢前，亦不去 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'reaching the front courtyard, he peered into the darkness where he could not see anything, hesitated, and did not dare to go forward, but also did not leave,' describing the scholar's reaction.

  • 字皆石镂 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the characters were all carved in stone,' indicating the material of the inscription.
  • 吟诵不已，不觉饥甚，呼道人觅食 - Narrative phrase meaning 'he recited it continuously, unaware that he was very hungry, and called out to the Taoist to find food,' describing the scholar's actions.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sentence '三人坐伞上，如坐椅桌，略不摇撼，但闻耳畔风声呼呼，如百万金甲衔枚赴关，巨浪洪涛汹涌而澎湃也' describes the sensation of sitting on an umbrella and the surrounding sounds and sights, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.
  • The sequence '俄顷已至，便令开目' indicates a quick arrival and the subsequent command to open the eyes, which must be read as a continuous action.
  • The compound description '妙女踏歌，游人如蚁，语言嘈杂，皆作吴音' must be read as a cohesive image, with each element contributing to the overall picture.
  • The sequence '时夜漏将残，真人笑曰：“可以归矣，”复令闭日，坐伞上如前' describes the late hour, the true person's laughter, and the command to close the eyes and sit on the umbrella again, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.
  • The sequence '从兄中途私计，必腾空也，试开目下视，则已堕地矣' describes the elder brother's private thoughts and the subsequent realization of their descent, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.
  • The sequence '宛转至晓，乃在广信人家茅屋上，扳缘而下' describes a long and winding journey, their final resting place, and their method of descent, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.
  • The sequence '忽记掌中画有钱，如真人教，每呵之，辄得一钱以市食，得不饥' describes the elder brother's realization and the magical solution to their hunger, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.
  • The sequence '五日至家，而画钱乃灭' describes their return home and the end of the magical effect, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.
  • The sequence '后有人自苏、扬返者，叩其所见，果不谬也' describes the verification of their experience, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.
  • The sequence '于是昆弟各异之' describes the end of their journey, requiring careful segmentation to maintain clarity.

  • 占沓作声 - Refers to the sound made by a stone when stepped on, often used to describe a noisy or disruptive sound.
